stem: A 30-year-old G2P2 woman is on the postnatal ward 12 hours after a normal vaginal birth at 39 weeks. Her antenatal course was complicated by gestational hypertension. She has a witnessed 2-minute generalised tonic-clonic seizure. After airway management and oxygen, she is loaded with magnesium sulfate 4 g IV over 15 minutes followed by an infusion of 1 g per hour. Twenty minutes later she has a second seizure. BP is 162/108 mmHg.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.9 degrees Celsius, HR 110, RR 18, BP 162/108 mmHg, SpO2 98% on 6 L oxygen via mask
data_table.Examination: Postictal, GCS 13 then improves to 15 over 15 minutes, brisk reflexes, no focal neurology, uterus well contracted with minimal lochia
data_table.Investigations: Magnesium level 1.8 mmol/L, platelets 132 x10^9/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, ALT 64 U/L, glucose 5.4 mmol/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Load with intravenous phenytoin 20 mg/kg as the magnesium has failed to prevent the second seizure
options.rationale: Phenytoin is not first-line for eclamptic recurrence; magnesium is more effective at a higher dose. Phenytoin is reserved for refractory seizures unresponsive to repeated magnesium boluses. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer a further 2 g magnesium sulfate intravenously over 5 to 10 minutes and increase the infusion rate
options.rationale: A recurrent eclamptic seizure on magnesium with a serum magnesium below the therapeutic range warrants a further 2 to 4 g bolus and increased infusion to reach 1.7 to 3.5 mmol/L target. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Cease magnesium sulfate, give intravenous calcium gluconate 1 g and commence intravenous diazepam infusion
options.rationale: Magnesium level is sub-therapeutic; calcium gluconate is reserved for magnesium toxicity (areflexia, respiratory depression). Benzodiazepines are not first-line for eclampsia. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange urgent non-contrast CT brain before any further antiseizure therapy is administered
options.rationale: Imaging is appropriate for atypical features or focal signs but should not delay magnesium bolus for ongoing eclampsia; treat the seizure first, image after stabilisation. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer intravenous labetalol 20 mg and intramuscular hydralazine 5 mg without further magnesium therapy
options.rationale: Blood pressure control is essential but does not treat the active seizure; magnesium must be re-bolused in addition to antihypertensive therapy. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: A recurrent eclamptic seizure on a magnesium sulfate regimen with sub-therapeutic serum level is managed with a further 2 to 4 g IV bolus and increased infusion rate to achieve serum levels of 1.7 to 3.5 mmol/L. Phenytoin and benzodiazepines are reserved for true refractory eclampsia after maximal magnesium. Calcium gluconate is for magnesium toxicity, not failure. Imaging is performed after stabilisation when focal features warrant.
key_points: Recurrent seizure on magnesium with sub-therapeutic level is rebolused with 2 to 4 g IV
Target serum magnesium is 1.7 to 3.5 mmol/L during maintenance infusion
Calcium gluconate is for toxicity (areflexia, respiratory depression), not seizure recurrence
explanation.pearls: Magnesium prophylaxis is continued for 24 hours after delivery OR last seizure (whichever is later); a postnatal eclamptic seizure resets this 24-hour clock.

stem: A 46-year-old man presents to his GP to quit smoking after 25 pack-years. He smokes 20 cigarettes per day and his Heaviness of Smoking Index is high. He has tried nicotine patches twice in the past without success. He has well-controlled hypertension on perindopril, no history of depression, suicidal ideation or seizures, and no recent cardiovascular events. He drinks 6 standard drinks per week. He asks for the most effective pharmacotherapy and is willing to try anything appropriate.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.6 degrees Celsius, HR 76, RR 14, BP 128/82 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: BMI 27 kg/m^2, no neurological abnormality, mood euthymic, no psychotic features
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 score 3 (minimal), AUDIT-C 4, fasting BSL 5.2 mmol/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Nicotine replacement therapy patch 21 mg daily monotherapy for 12 weeks with safety-net advice
options.rationale: Single-form NRT has lower quit rates than varenicline or combination NRT, and he has already failed patch monotherapy twice. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Bupropion sustained release 150 mg daily for 3 days then 150 mg twice daily for 12 weeks total duration
options.rationale: Bupropion is an alternative pharmacotherapy but varenicline has better quit rates; bupropion is preferred where varenicline is contraindicated or in dual smokers and depressed mood. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days then 1 mg twice daily for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Varenicline is the most effective single agent for smoking cessation in Australia (continuous abstinence around 25 percent), titrated up over the first week and continued for 12 weeks; it is suitable in this patient with no psychiatric or recent cardiovascular contraindications. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Clonidine 75 microgram twice daily titrated to tolerance as second-line nicotine cessation therapy
options.rationale: Clonidine is a second-line agent reserved for failure or intolerance of first-line therapies; it is not preferred initial pharmacotherapy. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Nortriptyline 25 mg nocte titrated to 75 mg daily as antidepressant-based smoking cessation therapy
options.rationale: Nortriptyline is a second-line agent and is less effective and less safe than varenicline; it is not first-line in Australian guidance. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Varenicline is the most effective single agent for smoking cessation in Australia and is recommended first-line where no contraindication exists. It is titrated over the first week and continued for 12 weeks. Combination NRT (patch plus oral) and bupropion are alternatives. Nortriptyline and clonidine are second-line. Behavioural support combined with pharmacotherapy further increases quit rates.
key_points: Varenicline 1 mg BD for 12 weeks is first-line in Australia
Combination NRT (patch plus oral) and bupropion are reasonable alternatives
Behavioural support with Quitline doubles the effect of pharmacotherapy
explanation.pearls: Earlier neuropsychiatric warnings about varenicline have been softened after the EAGLES trial; depression should be screened for before and during therapy but does not absolutely contraindicate use.

stem: A 38-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium carbonate 750 mg nocte for 4 years (steady-state level 0.7 mmol/L). She presents to her GP for routine review. She has noticed increasing thirst and polyuria over the last 6 months and is now drinking 4 L of fluid daily. She is otherwise well, sleeping normally with no mood symptoms. Her partner reports no behavioural change. She is concerned about her kidneys.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, HR 76, RR 14, BP 124/76 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Well, hydrated, no peripheral oedema, no tremor, thyroid not enlarged
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.68 mmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L (baseline 62), eGFR 84 mL/min, TSH 2.6 mIU/L, calcium 2.42 mmol/L, urine osmolality 220 mosmol/kg after overnight fluid restriction

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer to psychiatry for review of lithium dose and consider switching to an alternative mood stabiliser
options.rationale: Polyuria with reduced urine osmolality after fluid restriction in a lithium-treated patient indicates lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus; psychiatric review is needed to balance ongoing mood stabilisation against renal toxicity and consider dose reduction or switching. (Source: RANZCP Lithium Position Statement)

options.text: Ignore the symptoms because the lithium level is within range and continue routine 6-monthly testing
options.rationale: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus occurs in long-term lithium use regardless of therapeutic level and signals nephron damage; ignoring symptoms risks irreversible decline. (Source: RANZCP Lithium Position Statement)

options.text: Recommend further fluid restriction to under 1.5 L daily as treatment of polyuria from lithium therapy
options.rationale: Fluid restriction is dangerous in nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and risks hypernatraemic dehydration; treatment is to reduce or stop lithium under specialist supervision. (Source: RANZCP Lithium Position Statement)

options.text: Commence desmopressin 100 microgram nocte without specialist review for symptomatic relief at home
options.rationale: Desmopressin is largely ineffective in nephrogenic DI because the defect is renal tubular resistance, and specialist input is required before commencing. (Source: RANZCP Lithium Position Statement)

options.text: Increase lithium dose to 1000 mg nocte to maintain mood stability if level remains under 1.2 mmol/L
options.rationale: Increasing the lithium dose worsens renal effects; the current level is therapeutic and increasing it without reason is contraindicated, especially with emerging nephrogenic DI. (Source: RANZCP Lithium Position Statement)

explanation.summary: Polyuria and polydipsia in a long-term lithium-treated patient with reduced urine osmolality after fluid restriction indicate lithium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. Joint psychiatric and renal review is needed to consider dose reduction, switching to an alternative mood stabiliser, or adding amiloride. Fluid restriction is dangerous and desmopressin is largely ineffective because the defect is tubular resistance.
key_points: Lithium causes nephrogenic DI by reducing aquaporin-2 expression in collecting ducts
Long-term lithium also causes chronic interstitial nephritis and reduced eGFR
Joint psychiatric and renal review is required before dose change or switching
explanation.pearls: Amiloride 5 to 10 mg orally daily reduces lithium uptake in the principal cell and improves nephrogenic DI; thiazides can be used cautiously but raise lithium levels.

stem: A 28-year-old man presents to a psychiatrist with longstanding inattention, disorganisation and restlessness that have impaired his university and work performance since adolescence. Symptoms are present at home and at work. A structured clinical interview and ASRS confirm features of ADHD with onset in childhood. Substance use, mood and anxiety disorders are excluded. He has no personal or family history of cardiac disease, hypertension or seizure. He prefers a once-daily medication. ECG is normal.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, HR 72, RR 14, BP 122/76 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Mental state - mildly fidgety, mood euthymic, no psychotic features, insight preserved
data_table.Investigations: 12-lead ECG normal sinus rhythm, FBC, UEC and TSH all within normal limits

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Bupropion 150 mg sustained release each morning as off-label first-line therapy for adult ADHD
options.rationale: Bupropion has limited evidence in adult ADHD and is not endorsed as first-line in Australian guidelines. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Guidelines)

options.text: Atomoxetine 40 mg orally each morning titrated up to 80 mg over 4 weeks for symptom control
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is an alternative non-stimulant agent and is reserved for stimulant intolerance, substance use risk, or comorbid anxiety; it is not first-line. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Guidelines)

options.text: Immediate-release methylphenidate 10 mg three times daily as initial therapy for adult ADHD
options.rationale: Immediate-release stimulants are second-line in adults because of multiple daily doses and diversion risk; long-acting forms are preferred. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Guidelines)

options.text: Clonidine 75 microgram orally twice daily as a non-stimulant alternative to amphetamine-based therapy
options.rationale: Clonidine is used as augmentation in paediatric ADHD; it is not a first-line monotherapy in adults. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Guidelines)

options.text: Lisdexamfetamine 30 mg orally each morning, titrated up over 4 weeks to symptom response
options.rationale: Long-acting stimulants such as lisdexamfetamine are first-line for adult ADHD in Australia, offering once-daily dosing and lower abuse potential than immediate-release amphetamine. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD is treated first-line with a long-acting stimulant such as lisdexamfetamine or methylphenidate modified release in Australia. Pre-treatment cardiovascular assessment includes history, BP and ECG; ongoing monitoring tracks BP, pulse and weight. Non-stimulants (atomoxetine, guanfacine) are reserved for stimulant contraindication or intolerance.
key_points: Long-acting stimulants (lisdexamfetamine, methylphenidate MR) are first-line in adult ADHD
Pre-treatment cardiovascular history, BP and ECG are required before stimulant initiation
Non-stimulants are reserved for stimulant contraindication or substance use risk
explanation.pearls: Diversion risk should be discussed at every visit; in Australia, stimulant prescribing for adults is restricted and requires authority from the relevant state regulatory body.

sources.label: Australian Evidence-based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

stem: A 24-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for 2 years. She presents to her psychiatrist saying she is considering pregnancy in the next year. She is on no other psychotropic medications. Her last manic episode was 18 months ago; she has had no depressive episodes for 2 years. She is sexually active with one male partner using condoms. She has no other medical conditions.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.6 degrees Celsius, HR 72, RR 14, BP 116/74 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Mental state - euthymic, no psychotic or suicidal features, full insight
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 480 micromol/L, LFTs normal, FBC normal, beta-hCG negative

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Plan a cross-titration to lithium or lamotrigine with effective contraception until the new agent is established
options.rationale: Valproate has the highest teratogenic potential of mood stabilisers (about 10 percent major malformations, dose-related neurodevelopmental impact); women planning pregnancy are switched to a safer agent under cover of effective contraception. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guidelines)

options.text: Continue valproate at the current dose and recommend high-dose folate 5 mg daily for 3 months pre-conception
options.rationale: High-dose folate does not offset the malformation and neurodevelopmental risk of valproate; switching to a safer agent before conception is required. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guidelines)

options.text: Reduce valproate to 600 mg daily and continue this dose throughout pregnancy as a minimal teratogenic dose
options.rationale: There is no safe dose of valproate in pregnancy; even reduced doses carry a substantial risk of structural and neurodevelopmental harm. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guidelines)

options.text: Stop valproate immediately without cross-titration and review in 4 weeks for clinical mood relapse
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation risks relapse of bipolar disorder; cross-titration under cover of contraception is the safer pathway. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guidelines)

options.text: Insert a copper IUD and continue valproate indefinitely as long as effective contraception is maintained
options.rationale: While the pregnancy prevention program requires effective contraception, the woman is planning pregnancy and a switch is required, not indefinite continuation. (Source: TGA Valproate)

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate carries the highest teratogenic and neurodevelopmental risk among mood stabilisers. The risk is approximately 10 percent for major malformations and 30 to 40 percent for impaired cognitive development. Women of reproductive potential must be on a pregnancy prevention program. Women planning pregnancy are cross-titrated to a safer agent (lithium or lamotrigine) under cover of effective contraception before stopping contraception.
key_points: Valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy unless no effective alternative exists
Lithium and lamotrigine are the preferred mood stabilisers in pregnancy planning
Cross-titration occurs under cover of effective contraception, not abrupt cessation
explanation.pearls: Lamotrigine requires slow titration (25 mg/day increments) over 6 weeks to minimise the risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome; pre-conception planning should allow this lead time.
